metadata.notes: Content overhaul to Wingspan standard — Rogue B, March 2026. Research-corrected voyage structure, loot tokens, reputation overflow, score dial permanence. Sources: official Stonemaier rulebook, Dized official rules, BGG FAQ, r/boardgames strategy threads.

key_rulings.text: At the start of each voyage, deal 6 character cards to one player. All other players find and take the same 6 characters from their own decks.
Daytime abilities resolve from lowest rank to highest rank. Dusk abilities (loot collection) resolve from highest rank to lowest rank.
When two or more players play the same ranked character, the player with higher reputation resolves first.
Players keep unused character cards from previous voyages. Before each new voyage, 6 additional cards are dealt, so hands grow over the course of the game.
During dusk, you must take one loot token if any remain. You cannot voluntarily skip collecting loot.
Anchor abilities on characters in your ship resolve at the end of each voyage, after all days are complete.
Characters in every player's graveyard are visible to all players at all times.

At the end of each voyage, increase your score dial by your doubloon total, then discard those doubloons. The score dial total is permanent and cannot be lost. If your score exceeds 99 after the third voyage, set the dial to 99 and use doubloons to represent additional wealth.
Voyage 1 lasts 4 days, Voyage 2 lasts 5 days, and Voyage 3 lasts 6 days. The number of days increases each voyage regardless of player count.

If you gain more reputation than the track allows, gain 1 doubloon for each reputation you would have gained. If you lose more reputation than the track allows, lose 1 doubloon for each reputation you would have lost.
Each space on the reputation track displays a number representing doubloons earned by that player at the beginning of a new voyage, rewarding higher reputation positions.
Night abilities resolve after all loot has been collected during dusk. Night resolution order follows the same rank ordering as daytime.
All character abilities are mandatory. When a character's ability triggers at its designated time, you must resolve it — you cannot choose to skip it.

subtopics.title: Components
subtopics.content:
#### Board & Dials
- **1 double-sided island board** (508x406mm) — One side is **Calm**, the other is **Storm**. Each side shows the same day track and loot spaces, but loot token effects differ between sides. Calm is recommended for your first games; Storm adds more aggressive loot effects. The board works for all player counts (1-6).
- **6 score dials** — One per player. These circular dials track your doubloon total (can go negative). Rotate the dial to update your score.
- **6 graveyard tiles** — One per player. Place discarded characters face-up here. Everyone can see all graveyards.

#### Cards
- **240 character cards** (40 unique characters × 6 identical sets) — Each player gets their own complete set of 40 characters. Every character has a rank number (1-40), a name, and abilities that trigger at specific times of day.
- **39 Automa cards** — For solo play only.

#### Tokens
- **48 loot tokens** (bakelite) — Chunky tokens in 7 types, drawn from the cloth bag each voyage. Types: **Chest** (gold), **Map** (blue), **Amulet** (green), **Barrel** (brown), **Saber** (red), **Hook** (gray), **Relic** (purple/cursed). Effects depend on which side of the board you're playing (Calm or Storm).
- **7 loot tiles** — Reference tiles showing loot token effects.
- **6 reputation tokens** — One per player, placed on the reputation track on the board.
- **54 doubloon tokens** — Physical coins used alongside score dials for tracking.
- **4 Automa loot tiles** — For solo play only.

#### Other
- **1 midshipman token** — First-player marker shaped like a small figure.
- **1 cloth loot bag** — For drawing loot tokens randomly.
- **1 rulebook + 1 solo rulebook**

subtopics.title: Game Structure & Setup
subtopics.content:
#### How the Game Works
Libertalia is played over **3 voyages** of increasing length: **Voyage 1 = 4 days, Voyage 2 = 5 days, Voyage 3 = 6 days**. Every day, all players simultaneously select a character card from their hand and reveal. Characters resolve abilities and collect loot in rank order. After 3 voyages, the player with the most **doubloons** wins.

The twist: **all players have the same characters available**. You must outguess your opponents on which character to play and when.

#### Setting Up
1. **Place the island board** in the center — **Calm side up** for your first games, or **Storm side** for a more aggressive variant.
2. **Each player takes:** 1 complete set of 40 character cards (sorted by number), 1 score dial (set to 0 doubloons), 1 graveyard tile, and 1 reputation token.
3. **Place all reputation tokens** on the reputation track (6 spaces). Stack all tokens on the center space.
4. **Fill the loot bag** with all 48 loot tokens.
5. **Deal starting characters:** Randomly select 6 character cards from one player's deck. **All other players find the same 6 characters** in their decks. Everyone starts with the exact same 6-card hand.
6. **Prepare Voyage 1 loot:** Voyage 1 has 4 days. For each day, draw loot tokens from the bag equal to the number of players and place them face-up in the corresponding day row on the island board.
7. **Give the midshipman token** to a random player.

#### Checkpoint
Each player should have: the same 6 character cards in hand, a score dial at 0, a graveyard tile, and a reputation token on the track. The island board (Calm side up for first game) should show 4 rows of loot tokens — one row per day, with tokens equal to the player count in each row.

subtopics.title: The Day Phases
subtopics.content:
#### Each Day Has 5 Phases
Every day within a voyage follows the same sequence:

#### Phase 1: Sunrise — Select Characters
All players simultaneously choose **1 character card** from their hand and place it **face-down** in front of them. Once everyone is ready, all players reveal simultaneously.

If two or more players reveal the **same character** (same rank number), the player with **higher reputation** acts first among those tied characters.

#### Phase 2: Daytime — Day Abilities (Low → High)
Characters with a **daytime ability** (sun icon) resolve from **lowest rank to highest**. Low-rank characters act first in daytime — this is their advantage. Daytime abilities are often aggressive: stealing doubloons, discarding opponents' loot, or manipulating the board state. **All abilities are mandatory** — you cannot choose to skip them.

If a character is discarded or removed before their daytime ability triggers, their ability does **not** activate.

#### Phase 3: Dusk — Collect Loot (High → Low)
Starting with the **highest-ranked** character and going down, each player picks **exactly 1 loot token** from that day's row on the island. Higher-rank characters get first pick of the best loot — this is **their** advantage.

You **must** take a loot token if any remain. You cannot skip.

#### Phase 4: Night — Night Abilities
Characters with a **night ability** (moon icon) resolve, typically from lowest rank to highest. Night abilities often trigger based on what loot you collected or your current board state.

#### Phase 5: Rest
All played character cards move to each player's **ship** area (the space in front of them). Characters in your ship may have ongoing effects or anchor abilities that trigger at the end of the voyage. Proceed to the next day.

subtopics.title: Voyages & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### End of a Voyage
After all days in a voyage are complete, resolve the following in order:

1. **Anchor abilities** — Characters in your ship and loot tokens with an anchor icon (⚓) resolve their end-of-voyage abilities. You choose the order. These can grant bonus doubloons, penalize opponents, or score based on your collected loot.
2. **Score dial** — Add your current doubloon total to your **score dial**, then discard those physical doubloons. **The score dial total is permanent** — once doubloons are on the dial, they cannot be lost by any game effect.
3. **Discard loot** — Return all loot tokens to the bag (unless a character or loot ability says otherwise).
4. **Ship to graveyard** — Move all characters from your ship to your graveyard (face-up, visible to all). **Keep all remaining character cards in your hand** for the next voyage.
5. **Reputation income** — At the start of the next voyage, each player gains doubloons based on their position on the reputation track. Higher positions earn more.
6. **Deal new characters** — Deal 6 new characters using the same process: randomly select 6 from one player's remaining deck, and all players find and take those same 6.
7. **Prepare loot** — Draw loot tokens for the new voyage (5 rows for Voyage 2, 6 rows for Voyage 3).

#### Loot Token Types (Calm Side)
The 7 loot types and their Calm-side effects:
- **Chest** — Gain **5 doubloons** at end of voyage. The best straightforward loot.
- **Map** — Set collection: **7 doubloons per pair**, or **12 doubloons per set of 3**. Accumulating maps is very powerful.
- **Amulet** — Gain **3 doubloons** at end of voyage. Solid but less than a chest.
- **Barrel** — Gain **1 reputation** at end of the round, OR gain **1 doubloon** at end of voyage. Flexible.
- **Hook** — **Keep a character in your ship** for the next voyage (it doesn't go to the graveyard), OR gain **2 doubloons**. Strong for preserving powerful anchor abilities.
- **Saber** — **Discard another player's character** from the island track. Aggressive and situational.
- **Relic** — **Lose 3 doubloons** at end of voyage. The cursed loot — but some characters specifically benefit from having relics.

**Storm side** of the board changes these effects, making the game more aggressive and unpredictable.

**Growing Hands:** Players keep **unplayed character cards** from previous voyages. In Voyage 1, you start with 6 cards and play 4 (keeping 2). Before Voyage 2, you receive 6 new cards added to your kept cards. Before Voyage 3, you receive 6 more. Your hand grows, giving you more options but also more information about what opponents might play.

#### After Voyage 3
The game ends after the 3rd voyage's anchor abilities and score dial update. The player with the **most doubloons on their score dial wins**. If your score exceeds 99, set the dial to 99 and use doubloon tokens to represent additional wealth.

**Tiebreaker:** Higher reputation wins.

subtopics.title: Reputation & Graveyard
subtopics.content:
#### The Reputation Track
The reputation track has **6 spaces**. All player tokens are always on the track — when one moves right, others slide left to make room (and vice versa). Reputation serves three purposes:

1. **Tie-breaking during play** — When two players play the same ranked character on the same day, the player with higher reputation resolves first (in daytime) and picks loot first (at dusk).
2. **Voyage income** — Each space on the reputation track displays a doubloon number. At the start of each new voyage, you gain doubloons based on your track position. Higher reputation = more income.
3. **Final tiebreaker** — If two players end with the same doubloon total, higher reputation wins.

Reputation moves up or down based on character abilities and loot effects. Some characters reward high reputation; others benefit from low reputation.

**Overflow rule:** If you would gain reputation but are already at the rightmost space, gain **1 doubloon per reputation** you would have gained instead. If you would lose reputation at the leftmost space, **lose 1 doubloon per reputation** you would have lost.

#### The Graveyard
- Characters that leave your ship at the end of a voyage go to your **graveyard tile**, placed face-up.
- All graveyards are **public information** — any player can look at any graveyard at any time.
- Some characters have **graveyard abilities** that trigger when they enter the graveyard or based on what's already in it.
- Tracking opponents' graveyards is key to deduction — you can figure out which characters they still have in their deck by process of elimination.

#### Key Mechanism: Card Memory
Since all players receive the same characters each voyage, you can **track what opponents have played**. After Voyage 1, if an opponent played characters 5, 12, 18, and 23 (and kept 2), you know those 4 are in their graveyard and they still hold 2 unplayed cards from Voyage 1's deal — which you can narrow down.

This deduction gets richer each voyage as graveyards fill up and you gain more information about opponents' remaining hands.

subtopics.title: Beginner Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### The Core Tension: Rank vs. Timing
High-rank characters pick loot **first** (best selection) but resolve daytime abilities **last** (most vulnerable to disruption). Low-rank characters act **first** in daytime (powerful offensive abilities) but get **worst loot** at dusk.

**The decision every day:** Do you play high to get the best loot, or play low to strike first with a strong daytime ability?

#### Loot Reading
Before choosing your character, **always check what loot tokens are available** on today's row.
- If the loot is excellent (chests worth 5, maps for set collection), play a **high-rank character** to grab the best piece.
- If the loot is mediocre (barrels, amulets) or has relics (-3 doubloons), play a **low-rank character** with a strong daytime ability — you're not missing much at dusk, and you get to disrupt opponents.

#### The Mirroring Problem
Since all players have the same hand, everyone is looking at the same loot. If the loot is great, **everyone** wants to play high. This means your medium-rank character might actually get surprisingly good loot because all the high-rank characters cancel each other out on the pick order.

**Counter-play tip:** When loot is obviously good, consider playing a medium-rank card. Your opponents will fight over first pick with their highest cards, and you'll get a solid piece without burning your best character.

#### Saving vs. Spending
You keep unplayed characters between voyages. Every card you play is gone forever (to your ship, then graveyard). Every card you save carries forward.

- **Save your strongest characters** for high-value loot days or critical voyage moments.
- **Don't hoard everything** — unused characters score 0 doubloons. You must play cards to earn points.
- In Voyage 1 (6 cards, 4 days), you'll play 4 and keep 2. Voyage 2 (5 days) and Voyage 3 (6 days) get progressively longer, so manage your hand size carefully.

#### Reputation Matters
Reputation breaks ties when two players play the same rank. If you and an opponent both play character 22, higher reputation picks loot first. Move reputation up when you can — it provides consistent advantages in close games.

#### Relics Are Not Always Bad
Relics cost you 3 doubloons, but some characters specifically benefit from having relics. If your hand includes characters that profit from curses, deliberately collecting relics can be a strong contrarian strategy.

#### Reputation Is Income
Don't neglect reputation — it provides doubloon income at the start of each new voyage. Over 2 income phases (before Voyages 2 and 3), a high reputation position compounds significantly. Plus, overflow reputation converts directly to doubloons.

#### Watch the Graveyards
After each voyage, check what characters opponents have played. If you know someone used their strongest daytime-ability character already, you can play more aggressively on future days without fear of retaliation.

#### Common Mistakes
- **Always playing your highest card.** This is predictable and wastes strong characters on mediocre loot days.
- **Ignoring relics.** Taking a relic when no character in your hand benefits from it is -3 doubloons. But also don't forget — sometimes grabbing a relic to deny an opponent who DOES benefit from it is correct.
- **Ignoring reputation income.** Reputation isn't just a tiebreaker — it pays doubloons every voyage. Investing in reputation early pays dividends.
- **Not planning across voyages.** Characters you save in Voyage 1 compound with new cards in Voyage 2. Think ahead.
- **Forgetting anchor abilities.** Characters in your ship trigger anchor effects at voyage end — some of these are game-swingingly powerful. Plan around them.
- **Playing scared.** Sometimes playing a low-rank character to grab a curse before an opponent (who benefits from it) is the correct move.

subtopics.title: First-Game Walkthrough
subtopics.content:
#### Your First Game of Libertalia

**Setup**: Everyone receives the same 6 character cards. Look at your hand — you'll see 6 characters with rank numbers, names, and abilities marked with timing icons (sun, sunset, moon, anchor). The island board shows rows of face-up loot tokens — one row per day, with tokens equal to the number of players.

**Voyage 1, Day 1**: Look at the loot tokens in Day 1's row. Identify the best and worst pieces. Now look at your 6 characters. Pick one that matches the situation:
- Great loot available? Play a high-rank character to pick first.
- Mediocre loot? Play a low-rank character with a strong daytime ability.

Place your character face-down. Everyone reveals simultaneously. Resolve daytime abilities low-to-high, then collect loot high-to-low, then resolve night abilities.

**Days 2-4**: Repeat the process. Your hand shrinks each day. By Day 4, you have 2 cards left — play 1, keep 2 for next voyage.

**End of Voyage 1**: Resolve anchor abilities on characters in your ship. Resolve loot effects — chests add 5 doubloons each, maps score in sets, relics subtract 3 each. **Add your doubloon total to your score dial** (this total is now permanent), then discard physical doubloons. Move ship characters to your graveyard.

**Voyage 2**: Collect reputation income based on your track position. Deal 6 new characters (same process — everyone gets the same 6). You also keep your 2 unplayed cards from Voyage 1, so you start with 8 cards. Voyage 2 lasts **5 days** — longer than Voyage 1. New loot tokens are drawn for each day.

**Voyage 3**: Collect reputation income again. Deal 6 more new characters. Your hand may now be 9+ cards. Voyage 3 lasts **6 days** — the longest voyage. Maximum flexibility but also maximum deduction opportunity — check graveyards to deduce opponents' remaining hands.

**Final Scoring**: After Voyage 3's anchor abilities and score dial update, the player with the most doubloons on their score dial wins. First game target: 30-50 doubloons is solid depending on player count.
